In response to tne first question, any group whicn receives

advertisements.

litigat10n aimed at galning a place on the oallot for a 
candidate or a measure, aimed ac KeepIng a candIdate or 
measure off tne ballot, or cnallenging tne results of 
an election, the expendItures are mace for the purpose

or against a particular cand1date or measure and should

Thus, if a group receives over $500 in payments to qualify a

it must report all of its receipts and expenditures on tne Form 
420.

know the amount of tne expenditures incurred to date (for

RE: Payment for Qualification of Referendum 
Dear Ms. Moureaux: 
DAVID O. ROMANO, P.E. 
MICHAELS. WARDA 
Land Use Analysts 
This letter is in confmnation of our conversation today where you outlined the 
reporting requirements of a situation I described. A corporation has paid an amount in excess 
of $1,000.00 to a company to print petitions and gather signatures for a referendum in a 
California county. The corporation has not received contributions for this effort, but paid the 
entire amount itself. 
You informed me that the corporation qualified as a Major Donor Committee and 
would file Form 461 on a semi-annual basis. You further stated that should the corporation 
expend funds in the future for passage of the referendum, it may qualify as a Independent 
Expenditure Committee and thus file Form 465. 
The committee has no immediate filing 
requirements. For example, it need not file Form 410 within the 10 day period provided for that 
form. 
You were kind enough to put in the mail to me today Information Manual E: 
Independent Expenditure Committees and Major Donor Committees as well as the relevant forms. 


February 24, 1994 
Page 2 
Please infonn me if my understanding in this letter is incorrect in any way. Thank 
you for your assistance today.

sections.question: (1) Do groups which receive money and expend funds to qualify a ballot measure, to defend its qualification, or to promote or defeat its passage have reporting responsibilities? (2) Does an individual or entity which independently expends funds for legal fees to challenge the qualification of a ballot measure have a reporting obligation? (3) When should expenditures for attorney's fees and consulting fees be reported?
sections.conclusion: Any group receiving $500 or more in contributions for ballot measure qualification, defense, or passage/defeat must register as a recipient committee and report on Form 420. Individuals or entities making $500+ in independent expenditures to challenge a measure's qualification (including legal fees) must file as an independent expenditure committee on Forms 461 and 465. Committees must disclose attorney and consulting fees based on amounts known at filing time, or report as of the last monthly billing if amounts are unknown.
sections.question_synthetic: The document addresses three interrelated questions about campaign reporting obligations under California's Political Reform Act: whether citizen groups that receive contributions to qualify, defend, or promote/defeat ballot measures must report; whether individuals or entities independently spending money to challenge a measure's qualification must report; and when attorney and consulting fees should be reported.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that campaign reporting obligations apply to both groups receiving contributions for ballot measure activities (as recipient committees) and individuals/entities independently spending $500+ to challenge measure qualification (as independent expenditure committees). Attorney and consulting fees constitute reportable expenditures when tied to electoral purposes, and must be disclosed based on known amounts or latest monthly billing.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Primary document is the May 10, 1983 advice letter (A-83-096) to Brian Swift. A secondary document (Feb 24, 1994 correspondence from Normoyle & Newman confirming a phone conversation with FPPC staff) is appended but appears to be a confirmation letter rather than an FPPC response letter. Extraction focuses on the primary advice letter which contains clear QUESTION and CONCLUSION sections. The 1983 letter references the Buchanan Opinion (78-013) and applies it to the specific facts presented.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: (1) Do groups which receive money and expend funds to qualify a ballot measure, to defend its qualification, or to promote or defeat its passage have reporting responsibilities? (2) Does an individual or entity which independently expends funds for legal fees to challenge the qualification of a ballot measure have a reporting obligation? (3) When should expenditures for attorney's fees and consulting fees be reported?

CONCLUSION: Any group receiving $500 or more in contributions for ballot measure qualification, defense, or passage/defeat must register as a recipient committee and report on Form 420. Individuals or entities making $500+ in independent expenditures to challenge a measure's qualification (including legal fees) must file as an independent expenditure committee on Forms 461 and 465. Committees must disclose attorney and consulting fees based on amounts known at filing time, or report as of the last monthly billing if amounts are unknown.

embedding.summary: In this May 10, 1983 advice letter responding to the City Attorney of Pleasanton, the FPPC addresses campaign reporting obligations for groups involved in ballot measures, including those receiving contributions to qualify measures and those independently spending funds for litigation challenging a measure's qualification. The letter clarifies that such groups qualify as either recipient committees or independent expenditure committees with specific Form filing requirements.
